title: Finding the range
problem: Find the range of the dataset: $8, \ 3, \ 15, \ 11, \ 6, \ 19, \ 4$

steps.instruction: Identify the highest and lowest values.
Apply the formula: Range = highest − lowest.

title: Finding the interquartile range
problem: Find the interquartile range (IQR) of the ordered dataset: $5, \ 8, \ 12, \ 15, \ 18, \ 22, \ 27$

steps.working: There are 7 values. The median is the 4th value: $15$.

steps.instruction: Identify the lower half (values below the median) and find $Q_1$.
steps.working: Lower half: $\{5, 8, 12\}$. The median of this half is $Q_1 = 8$.

steps.instruction: Identify the upper half (values above the median) and find $Q_3$.
steps.working: Upper half: $\{18, 22, 27\}$. The median of this half is $Q_3 = 22$.

title: Comparing range and IQR when an outlier is present
problem: For the dataset $6, \ 8, \ 10, \ 12, \ 14, \ 16, \ 52$, calculate the range and the IQR, and explain which better describes the spread.

steps.instruction: Find the median to split the data.
steps.working: There are 7 values. The median is the 4th value: $12$.

steps.instruction: Calculate the IQR and compare with the range.
steps.working: $\text{IQR} = 16 - 8 = 8$. The range is $46$ because the outlier $52$ pulls the maximum far from the rest. The IQR is only $8$, reflecting the true spread of the central data. The IQR is the better measure here.

answer: Range $= 46$; IQR $= 8$. The IQR better describes the spread because it is not distorted by the outlier $52$.
